text.title: API Patterns, Caching Strategies, and Background Jobs
text.summary: Consistent API response envelope, auth pattern selection matrix, cache-aside with stampede guard, and Celery job pattern.

rule.statement: Centralize the API response envelope in one module (Pydantic model or TypedDict); success responses wrap data under a data key, errors under an error key with code, message, and details.
rule.rationale: Inconsistent envelope shapes force every API consumer to handle different structures; a single module makes the shape a code-reviewable contract that prevents drift.

rule.statement: Choose auth pattern based on statefulness requirement: JWT for stateless API auth, session cookies for server-side state, OAuth2 for third-party login, API keys for server-to-server.
rule.rationale: Using JWT where session revocation is needed (e.g., security incident) requires a token blacklist, which negates the stateless advantage.

rule.statement: Never let an agent rotate production signing keys, OAuth client secrets, or password-hash parameters; those changes require human approval and coordinated deployment.
rule.rationale: Auth key rotation is a coordinated security operation; autonomous rotation without a deployment window invalidates all active sessions and can lock users out.

rule.statement: For cache-aside (the most common pattern), always pair set+get with a documented TTL, jitter (TTL ± 10%), and a single-flight guard against stampede.
rule.rationale: Without jitter, a cache expiry wave causes all callers to miss simultaneously and hammer the database. Without single-flight, N concurrent misses each trigger a DB query.

antipattern.description: Agent "fixing" a stale cache read by lowering TTL instead of adding a write-through invalidation on the write path.
antipattern.reason: Lower TTL increases cache miss rate and database load; the root cause is a missing write-path invalidation. Fix the write path, not the TTL.

rule.statement: Store idempotency keys and created_at on every background job; design job handlers to be idempotent so failed jobs can be re-driven without duplication.
rule.rationale: Networks and workers fail; a non-idempotent job re-driven after failure charges customers twice or sends duplicate emails.
